title: AI Workloads & Capabilities Identification
blueprintRef: Identify AI concepts and capabilities (40-45%) > Identify AI workloads
source: Concepts 1, 3, 4, 5, 6 (NLP, Speech, Computer Vision, Information Extraction)

questions.stem: A marketing team wants to monitor social media posts and automatically classify each post as positive, negative, or neutral about their brand. Which AI workload best fits?

explanation.correct: Classifying text as positive/negative/neutral is sentiment analysis, a text-analysis (NLP) technique.

distractors.B: Computer vision analyses images, not text posts.
distractors.C: Speech recognition converts audio to text, not relevant to written posts.
distractors.D: Information extraction pulls structured fields from documents, not sentiment.

questions.stem: A call centre wants to convert recorded customer phone calls into written transcripts. Which workload is required?

explanation.correct: Converting spoken audio into text is speech recognition (speech-to-text, STT).

distractors.B: Text-to-speech goes the other direction (text to audio).
distractors.C: Sentiment analysis could run on the transcript afterwards, but transcription itself is STT.
distractors.D: OCR reads text from images, not audio.

questions.stem: An accessibility feature reads on-screen articles aloud for visually impaired users in a natural-sounding voice. Which workload is this?

explanation.correct: Generating audible speech from text is speech synthesis (text-to-speech, TTS).

distractors.B: STT converts audio to text, the opposite.
distractors.C: Summarisation shortens text, it doesn't vocalise it.

questions.stem: A retailer wants to detect which products are on a shelf and where each one is located in the image (with bounding boxes). Which computer vision task fits?

explanation.correct: Identifying individual objects AND their locations via bounding boxes is object detection.

distractors.B: Image classification predicts one label for the whole image, no locations.
distractors.C: Semantic segmentation labels every pixel (precise masks), more than 'where each product is' by box.
distractors.D: Image generation creates new images, not detection.

questions.stem: An app needs to predict whether a single uploaded photo shows a cat, a dog, or a bird (one main label). Which task is this?

explanation.correct: Predicting the single main subject label of an image is image classification.

distractors.B: Object detection also gives locations of multiple objects.

questions.stem: An accounts team wants to automatically pull the invoice number, date, vendor, and total from scanned supplier invoices into a database. Which workload fits best?

explanation.correct: Extracting structured fields (invoice number, date, total) from documents is information extraction (OCR + field mapping).

distractors.B: Sentiment analysis judges tone, not fields.
distractors.C: Classification labels the whole image, not individual fields.

questions.stem: A designer types 'a watercolour fox in a snowy forest' and the system produces a brand-new image. Which workload is this?

explanation.correct: Producing new images from a text prompt is image generation, a generative AI workload (diffusion).

distractors.C: Object detection locates objects in existing images.
distractors.D: Segmentation masks pixels in existing images.

questions.stem: A company wants a conversational assistant that answers customer questions in natural language and can hold a back-and-forth dialogue. Which workload is this?

explanation.correct: A natural-language conversational assistant is a generative AI (chat) workload.

distractors.B: Information extraction pulls fields from documents.

questions.stem: A support desk receives tickets in many languages and wants to automatically identify which language each ticket is written in before routing. Which text-analysis technique is this?

explanation.correct: Identifying the language of a document (often the first step in a pipeline) is language detection.

distractors.C: Key phrase extraction pulls main concepts.

questions.stem: A legal firm needs to automatically find and redact names, addresses, and phone numbers in documents before sharing them. Which technique addresses this?

explanation.correct: Finding and optionally redacting personal data (names, addresses, phones) is PII (personally identifiable information) detection.

distractors.C: Image classification is a vision task.

questions.stem: A medical imaging tool must outline the exact pixels belonging to a tumour in a scan, producing a precise mask rather than a box. Which computer vision task is this?

explanation.correct: Classifying individual pixels by object to create precise masks is semantic segmentation.

distractors.B: Object detection gives bounding boxes, not pixel-precise masks.
distractors.C: Classification gives one label for the whole image.

questions.stem: An executive wants long internal reports condensed into a few key points while keeping the main message. Which technique fits?

explanation.correct: Reducing text volume while keeping the main points is summarisation.

distractors.C: Object detection is a vision task.

questions.stem: A product team wants to pull out the main concepts and talking points from thousands of customer reviews (e.g., 'battery life', 'screen quality'). Which technique fits?

explanation.correct: Identifying the main concepts/talking points from text is key phrase extraction.

distractors.B: Sentiment analysis would judge whether reviews are positive/negative, not extract topics.

questions.stem: A news aggregator wants to automatically tag each article with the people, organisations, and places it mentions. Which technique is this?

explanation.correct: Finding people, organisations, places, dates and quantities in text is entity detection (NER).

distractors.D: Image classification is a vision task.

questions.stem: A solution must understand a user's goal, then use tools to look up flights, book one, and email a confirmation, deciding the steps itself. Which workload best describes this?

explanation.correct: A system that reasons toward a goal and uses tools to take multi-step actions is an AI agent (agentic AI = model + instructions + tools).

questions.stem: A studio types a prompt and the system produces a short moving video clip that obeys physical motion and a logical sequence of events. Which workload is this?

explanation.correct: Creating new video from a prompt, accounting for physical behaviour and temporal progression, is video generation.

distractors.B: Object detection finds objects in existing media.
distractors.C: Segmentation masks pixels in existing images.
distractors.D: Speech recognition handles audio to text.

questions.stem: An app receives a photo and returns a natural-language sentence describing the scene, e.g., 'A person in a park with a hat and a backpack.' Which capability is this?

explanation.correct: Generating a natural-language description of an image is a multimodal capability (vision + language).

distractors.B: Classification returns a single label, not a descriptive sentence.
distractors.C: OCR reads text within an image.
distractors.D: Object detection returns objects + boxes, not a fluent description.

questions.stem: An archive wants to digitise thousands of handwritten census forms so the text becomes searchable. Which workload is the starting point?

explanation.correct: Converting text in scanned/handwritten documents into machine-readable text is OCR, the basis of information extraction.

questions.stem: A voice assistant in a car lets the driver speak a command and replies out loud. Which TWO workloads combine here?

explanation.correct: Listening to a spoken command (STT) and replying aloud (TTS) combines speech recognition and speech synthesis.

distractors.B: OCR/classification are vision tasks, not voice.
distractors.D: These are text-analysis techniques, not the listen+speak loop.

questions.stem: A helpdesk wants to automatically assign each incoming text ticket to a category such as 'billing', 'technical', or 'account'. Which technique is this?

explanation.correct: Assigning text to predefined categories is text classification (sentiment analysis is one special case of it).

distractors.B: Key phrase extraction pulls concepts, it doesn't assign a category.
distractors.D: Object detection is a vision task.

questions.stem: What does sentiment analysis do?

options.text: Classifies text as positive, negative, or neutral
Detects the language of a document

explanation.correct: Sentiment analysis classifies the emotional tone of text as positive/negative/neutral.

questions.stem: What is key phrase extraction?

options.text: Identifying the main concepts/talking points in text
Reducing a document to a shorter version

explanation.correct: Key phrase extraction identifies the main concepts in unstructured text.

questions.stem: Entity detection (NER) is used to identify which of the following in text?

options.text: People, places, organisations, dates, and quantities
The language the text is written in
The pixels belonging to each object

explanation.correct: Entity detection finds named entities such as people, places, organisations, dates and quantities.

distractors.D: That is semantic segmentation (a vision task).

questions.stem: What distinguishes abstractive summarisation from extractive summarisation?

options.text: Abstractive generates new wording; extractive selects existing sentences
Abstractive selects existing sentences; extractive generates new wording
Both invent entirely new facts not in the source

explanation.correct: Abstractive summarisation generates new language summarising themes; extractive summarisation selects existing sentences (e.g., TextRank).

distractors.D: A good summariser should not invent facts; this is not the distinction.

questions.stem: What is the key difference between image classification and object detection?

options.text: Classification predicts one label for the whole image; object detection finds multiple objects and their locations
Classification finds object locations; object detection predicts one whole-image label
Both generate new images from prompts

explanation.correct: Classification = one label for the main subject; object detection = locate multiple objects with bounding boxes.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly describes speech-to-text vs text-to-speech?

options.text: STT converts audio to text; TTS converts text to audio
STT converts text to audio; TTS converts audio to text

explanation.correct: Speech recognition (STT) = audio to text; speech synthesis (TTS) = text to audio.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of optical character recognition (OCR)?

options.text: Detect and read text that appears within images or scanned documents
Classify the sentiment of a sentence

explanation.correct: OCR detects and reads text within images/scanned documents, the first step of information extraction.

questions.stem: Which best distinguishes object detection from semantic segmentation?

options.text: Object detection draws bounding boxes around objects; segmentation labels every pixel belonging to each object
Object detection labels every pixel; segmentation draws bounding boxes
Both only return a single whole-image label
Both only work on text, not images

explanation.correct: Object detection = bounding boxes; semantic segmentation = pixel-level masks.

distractors.C: A single whole-image label is classification.

questions.stem: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is used in a generative AI solution primarily to do what?

options.text: Retrieve relevant documents and add them to the prompt so responses are grounded in your data
Convert speech to text before answering
Compress a model to run on a device

explanation.correct: RAG retrieves relevant source documents and augments the prompt, grounding the model's response in your data.

distractors.D: That describes model optimisation/SLMs, not RAG.

questions.stem: How does information extraction differ from general text analysis?

options.text: Information extraction pulls structured field values from documents/forms (often via OCR first); text analysis interprets meaning within already-digital text
Information extraction only works on audio
Text analysis only works on images

explanation.correct: Information extraction turns unstructured content (scanned forms, images) into structured fields, typically starting with OCR; text analysis interprets meaning in text that is already digital.

distractors.B: They overlap but are distinct workloads.
distractors.C: Information extraction works on documents, images, and increasingly audio/video, not only audio.
distractors.D: Text analysis works on text, not images.

questions.stem: A bank wants to flag, within free-text complaints, every mention of an account number and customer name. This is closest to which technique?

explanation.correct: Finding specific entities like names and identifiers in text is entity detection, and when it includes personal data, PII detection.

distractors.C: Classification is a vision task here / whole-item labelling.

questions.stem: Which scenario is best solved by a generative AI workload rather than a traditional classification model?

options.text: Drafting a unique personalised email reply from a short brief
Predicting whether an image is a cat or a dog
Detecting the language of a sentence
Locating cars in a traffic photo

explanation.correct: Producing new, original content (a drafted email) is a generative AI task.

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following are computer vision tasks? (Choose two.)

explanation.correct: Object detection and semantic segmentation are computer vision tasks.

distractors.C: Sentiment analysis is a text-analysis technique.
distractors.D: Language detection is a text-analysis technique.

questions.stem: Which THREE of the following are text-analysis (NLP) techniques? (Choose three.)

explanation.correct: Key phrase extraction, entity detection, and sentiment analysis are all text-analysis techniques.

distractors.D: Object detection is a computer vision task.
distractors.E: Image generation is a generative AI / vision task.

questions.stem: Which TWO scenarios are best addressed by generative AI? (Choose two.)

options.text: Writing a first draft of a blog post from bullet points
An assistant that summarises and rewrites a document into plain language
Counting how many red pixels are in an image
Measuring the loudness of an audio clip
Detecting the language of a paragraph

explanation.correct: Drafting new content (A) and rewriting/summarising into new language (B) are generative AI tasks.

distractors.C: Counting pixels is basic image processing, not generative AI.
distractors.E: Language detection is a deterministic text-analysis technique.

questions.stem: Case study: A retail chain is building several AI features. (1) It wants to automatically describe product photos in plain English for its catalogue. (2) It wants to pull line items and totals from scanned supplier receipts into its finance system. (3) It wants a chat assistant that answers shopper questions conversationally.

subQuestions.stem: Feature (1) — describe product photos in plain English — needs which capability?

explanation.correct: Generating natural-language descriptions of images is a multimodal computer vision capability.

distractors.C: PII detection finds personal data in text.
distractors.D: Object detection returns boxes, not fluent descriptions.

subQuestions.stem: Feature (2) — pull line items and totals from scanned receipts — needs which workload?

explanation.correct: Extracting structured fields from scanned documents is information extraction (OCR + field mapping).

subQuestions.stem: Feature (3) — a conversational shopper assistant — is which workload?

explanation.correct: A conversational natural-language assistant is a generative AI (chat) workload.

questions.stem: Case study: A hospital is adding AI features. (1) Doctors dictate notes aloud and want them typed into the patient record. (2) The system should read discharge instructions aloud for patients with low vision. (3) Staff want to extract structured fields from scanned referral forms.

subQuestions.stem: Feature (1) — dictation typed into the record — needs which workload?

explanation.correct: Turning spoken dictation into text is speech recognition (STT).

distractors.D: Object detection is a vision task.

subQuestions.stem: Feature (2) — read instructions aloud for low-vision patients — needs which workload?

explanation.correct: Reading text aloud is speech synthesis (TTS); it also supports the Inclusiveness principle.

subQuestions.stem: Feature (3) — extract structured fields from scanned referral forms — needs which workload?

explanation.correct: Pulling structured fields from scanned forms is information extraction.

questions.stem: A security system must recognise and verify a specific individual's face to grant building access. Which broad workload does this fall under?

explanation.correct: Facial recognition analyses visual input and is a computer vision workload.

distractors.C: Information extraction pulls fields from documents.

questions.stem: An email provider wants to automatically label messages as 'spam' or 'not spam' based on their wording. Which workload/technique is this?

explanation.correct: Labelling text into categories (spam/not spam) is text classification, a text-analysis technique.

distractors.D: Object detection is a vision task.

questions.stem: A team needs to extract spoken action items from recorded meeting AUDIO. Which combination of workloads is most appropriate?

options.text: Speech recognition to transcribe, then text analysis/summarisation to extract action items
OCR to read the audio, then image generation

explanation.correct: Audio must first be transcribed (speech recognition), then text analysis/summarisation extracts the action items.

distractors.B: OCR reads images, not audio; image generation is irrelevant.
distractors.C: These are vision tasks unrelated to audio.
distractors.D: TTS produces audio; image classification is unrelated.
